---
title: "Homework: Day 1 → Day 2"
subtitle: "Due at the Start of Day 2"
date: "Estimated time: 1.5 hours"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
---

::: {.callout-tip icon=true}
## Do this assignment in Colab

The interactive version with answer cells lives in a Colab notebook (R kernel — Q2 uses limma + qvalue):
[**Open Homework in Colab**](https://colab.research.google.com/github/pflahert/hd-stats-workshop/blob/main/notebooks/homework.ipynb)

In Colab: **Runtime → Change runtime type → R** before running cells.

This page is the readable web reference.
:::

## Purpose

This homework consolidates Day 1 material and bridges to Day 2. It maps onto the Day-1 structure:

- **Q1 — Multiple Testing Concepts (LO1, LO3)**. Exercises **Lecture 1** (hypothesis testing: Bonferroni / FWER, BH / FDR, $q$-values) and includes an **AI explanation + critique** exercise.
- **Q2 — FDR in Practice (LO1, LO3)**. Connects both Day-1 lectures: BH-adjusted $p$-values from **Lecture 1**; `limma`'s empirical-Bayes variance shrinkage and $\hat\pi_0$ estimation from **Lecture 2**. **All three sub-parts ask you to use AI for code generation** and to critique what comes back.
- **Q3 — Evaluating Analysis Output (LO3)**. Exercises the critical-evaluation thread from both lectures, with explicit **you-vs-AI comparison** exercises in writing specifications, debugging, and code review.

**AI integration is required.** Every question has at least one sub-part where you direct an AI assistant, paste its output, and evaluate it against named criteria. Use any of: the [UMass campus GenAI platform](https://www.umass.edu/it/genai/platform), Claude, ChatGPT, or another assistant of your choice. Note which AI you used for each prompt — different models behave differently, and the differences are themselves part of the lesson.

Conceptual and interpretation questions must reflect your own understanding. The "AI's response" sections record what the AI produced; the surrounding critique cells record *your* evaluation of it.

---

## Q1: Multiple Testing Concepts (LO1, LO3)

*Exercises Lecture 1 (Bonferroni / FWER and BH / FDR).*

A proteomics experiment measures the abundance of $p = 5{,}000$ proteins in $n = 30$ tissue samples (15 tumor, 15 normal). For each protein, a two-sample $t$-test is performed.

**(a)** Under the global null (no protein is differentially abundant), how many proteins would you expect to reject at $\alpha = 0.05$ without any correction?

**(b)** The Bonferroni-corrected threshold is $\alpha / m$. Compute this threshold. A protein with a "real" effect has a $p$-value of $3 \times 10^{-4}$. Would it survive Bonferroni correction?

**(c)** You apply the BH procedure at FDR $= 0.10$ and get 120 rejections. Give an interpretation of this result in plain language that a biologist would understand.

**(d)** Your collaborator says "We found 120 significant proteins." A reviewer replies "But 12 of those are probably false positives!" Is the reviewer being unreasonable? Explain.

**(e) AI explanation + critique.** Ask an AI assistant the following prompt:

> "Explain the difference between FWER (Bonferroni-style) and FDR (Benjamini–Hochberg-style) control to a wet-lab biologist who has never taken a statistics course. Use the analogy of finding stars in a noisy telescope image. Three short paragraphs at most."

Paste the AI's response. Then critique it against five criteria: (i) Does it define both FWER and FDR correctly? (ii) Does it explain the key difference (*any* vs. *fraction*)? (iii) Does it pick the right context (FDR for genomic discovery)? (iv) Does it avoid the common error of saying "FDR is the probability that a finding is false"? (v) Is the star/telescope analogy accurate? Conclude with a one-sentence overall assessment: would you use this explanation when introducing the concept to a wet-lab collaborator?

---

## Q2: FDR in Practice (LO1, LO3)

*Connects both Day-1 lectures: `limma`'s `eBayes` is the empirical-Bayes variance shrinkage from Lecture 2; BH adjustment and Storey's $\hat\pi_0$ are from Lecture 1. All three sub-parts use AI to generate the R code; the critique sections check it.*

Using the Golub dataset (introduced in Lecture 1 and used in Labs 1 and 2), perform the following analysis. You should use AI to write the R code, but evaluate its correctness and interpret the results yourself.

**(a) BH-adjusted p-values via `limma`.** Direct an AI to compute BH-adjusted $p$-values for the ALL vs. AML comparison **using `limma`** (not raw $t$-tests). Report the number of genes significant at FDR $< 0.01$, $< 0.05$, $< 0.10$.

Suggested prompt (paste-ready, with R-specific format directive):

> "Reply with a single R code block (no prose, no .docx file — just runnable code I can paste into an R-kernel Colab cell). Using `limma` with empirical-Bayes variance shrinkage, fit the ALL vs. AML contrast on the Golub expression matrix `expr` (3,051 genes × 72 samples) using subtype labels from `meta`, extract BH-adjusted p-values from `topTable(..., adjust.method = 'BH', number = Inf)`, and print the number of genes with `adj.P.Val` below 0.01, 0.05, and 0.10."

Include the prompt you used and note whether the AI spontaneously chose `limma` or whether you had to direct it.

**(b) Estimate $\hat\pi_0$ with `qvalue`.** Use the `qvalue` package to estimate $\hat{\pi}_0$. What does this estimate tell you about the dataset?

Suggested prompt:

> "Reply with a single R code block (no prose, no .docx file). Given the vector of raw p-values `pvals` from limma's topTable on the Golub data, use `qvalue::qvalue(pvals)` to estimate pi_0. Print pi_0_hat with three decimal places and a one-line interpretation of what that value implies about the proportion of genes that are non-null. Do not invent or hallucinate numbers — only print what qvalue returns."

**(c) Annotated p-value histogram.** Make a $p$-value histogram. Annotate it: mark the region where you think the true alternatives are concentrated, and the region dominated by true nulls.

Suggested prompt:

> "Reply with a single R code block (no prose, no .docx file). Given the vector of raw p-values `pvals` from limma's topTable on the Golub data and the qvalue-estimated `pi_0`, produce a `ggplot2` histogram of the p-values with 50 bins (density scaled), overlay a horizontal dashed reference line at the uniform-null density (height 1), overlay a horizontal dotted line at the estimated null-density height pi_0, and annotate two regions: a vertical band 0-0.1 labelled 'spike of true alternatives', and a vertical band 0.5-1.0 labelled 'roughly uniform null region.' Use clear axis labels and a title."

For each of (a), (b), and (c): paste the AI's code, note any corrections you had to make, and include a one-paragraph interpretation of the results in your own words.

---

## Q3: Evaluating Analysis Output (LO3)

*Exercises the critical-evaluation thread from both Day-1 lectures and labs. Three of the four sub-parts include explicit you-vs-AI comparisons.*

**(a)** Write two specifications for the same analysis task: "Perform differential expression analysis on a microarray dataset comparing treatment and control groups."

- **Specification A**: Vague enough that it would likely produce an incorrect or incomplete analysis.
- **Specification B**: Precise enough that it would likely produce a correct analysis on the first attempt. Name the method, the test, the multiple-testing correction, and the threshold.

Explain what makes Specification B better.

**(b)** You read (in a paper or AI output) the claim: "Gene XYZ promotes cell proliferation in breast cancer through the PI3K/AKT pathway (Smith et al., 2019)." Describe the steps you would take to verify this before including it in your own work.

**(c)** Describe one specific error you encountered during today's labs—whether in AI-generated code, your own code, or a collaborator's analysis. What was the error? How did you catch it? What statistical knowledge was required to recognize it?

Suggested AI prompt for follow-up (paste-ready, with R-specific format directive):

> "Reply with a single R code block (no prose, no .docx file). Here is a snippet of analysis code: [paste your snippet]. Identify the most likely statistical error and write a corrected version of the snippet. If the original is correct, return the same code unchanged with a one-line comment confirming this."

**(d) A short review — you vs. AI.** You receive a complete differential expression analysis from a collaborator. It uses raw $t$-tests with BH correction on a microarray dataset with 72 samples. Write a short review (3–4 sentences) describing what's adequate and what should be changed, and why. *(Hint: connect this to Lecture 2's argument that `limma`'s empirical-Bayes variance shrinkage is preferred over raw $t$-tests when $n$ is small. The [Decision Guide](../syllabus/decision-guide.qmd) summarizes the recommended methods.)*

Now ask an AI for *its* review of the same scenario:

> "Reply in three to four sentences only (no .docx file). A collaborator submitted a differential expression analysis on a microarray dataset with $n = 72$ samples using raw two-sample $t$-tests with Benjamini–Hochberg FDR correction. Write a brief critical review explaining what is adequate about this approach and what should be changed, and why."

Compare your review to the AI's: what did the AI catch that you missed; what did you catch that the AI missed; did either raise false alarms; which review would you submit if you could only pick one?

---

## Submission

Bring your written answers and code output (for Q2) to Day 2. Be prepared to reference your answers during Lecture 3 and Lecture 4 discussions.

### Assessment Criteria

This is a formative assessment. Completeness and engagement matter more than getting every detail right. We are particularly interested in your interpretation of statistical results in your own words and your ability to identify errors in analysis code or output.
